comments.content:
Cool demo, but the core question is incentives. Who actually pays here and why? Organizers pay if this reduces judge time, deconflicts schedules, and lowers fights about bias; sponsors pay if the theatrical reveal and British voice turn judging into on-stage content that drives brand recall and lead capture. The risk is incentive distortion problem-solution fit. That is not necessarily bad if the buyer wants spectacle and consistent scoring, but it is misaligned with “build something valuable.” Publish the rubric, show calibration data, keep a human veto, and make consent and data rights explicit since you are recording, processing, and storing demos. Reputational risk management is what buyers are actually purchasing.

Unit economics look serviceable. Back-of-envelope10 minutes is ~4 hours of AV, three parallel model passes, TTS, and storage. That is likely tens to a low few hundred dollars in variable cost, but the real cost center is ops reliability and on-site operator time. So the business feels like a per-event license with sponsorable assets and upsells per team. Example10k event fee. Price what it saves or what it sells, not what the APIs cost.

comments.content:
Back when forums ran on phpBB and vBulletin, moderation was a couple of stubborn volunteers, a visible rules page, and a public ban log. If a thread went off the rails, an op would lock it, post a short note, maybe DM you a warning, and the whole drama lived in one place. Even Slashdot had meta-moderation so overzealous mods got checked. It was messy and imperfect, but the scale matched the tools and the consequences were legible. Example stood, and you could see the reasoning.

Today’s Trust and Safety is industrial process control. The unspoken secret is that policy is written by PR and legal, enforced by contractors on dashboards with 20-second SLAs, and bent by escalation paths for VIPs and news cycles. Shadow actions are preferred over outright bans because they’re less screenshot-able. The same post can be satire on Monday, harassment on Friday, depending on region, advertiser pressure, and current crisis. Not a grand conspiracy, just incentives turning judgment into throughput.

posts.title: Unified Spatio-Temporal Token Scoring for Efficient Video VLMs

comments.content: Interesting, but is this really new compared to ToMe, DynamicViT, EViT, TokenLearner, or recent LLaVA/Video-LLaMA pruning that uses cross-modal saliency? Many works already prune or merge tokens in ViTs and some do LLM-side selection; what’s fundamentally different about scoring spatial tokens via LLM gradients without explicit text conditioning? The 50% prune for ~0.7% drop sounds very similar to prior prune/merge tradeoffs; is the gain mostly from the packing trick? Also curious how this holds outside video QA, and whether the end-to-end gradients remain stable on longer contexts and higher frame rates.

comments.content:
Another day, another paper chasing "efficiency" in AI models, as if pruning tokens in video VLMs is going to save us from the bloated mess of modern tech. This obsession with squeezing out marginal gains—62% faster training by dropping half the tokens, with just a tiny performance hit—feels like a symptom of deeper rot. We're in an era where institutions prioritize quick hacks over foundational breakthroughs, churning out incremental tweaks while ignoring the human cost. It's all hype, assuming we'll keep scaling forever without addressing the energy suck or the ethical voids. Mediocrity reigns; real innovation gets drowned in arXiv noise.

But let's push back: serious work, the kind driven by human creativity, still matters. Think of early computer vision pioneers who built from scratch without relying on massive datasets or token tricks—they solved problems with elegant math, not just optimization gimmicks. Defending that ethos means valuing depth over speed, fostering cultures that reward genuine insight. If we don't, this decline into competent-but-soulless tech will only accelerate, leaving us with efficient voids instead of meaningful progress.

comments.content:
The core question here is whether anyone actually needs this particular optimization badly enough to retrain their models or rebuild their infrastructure around it. Token pruning saves compute, which translates to lower serving costs and potentially faster user-facing latency, but the value proposition depends entirely on deployment scale and margin structure. If you're running a consumer video Q&A product at million-query scale, shaving 62% off compute per request might justify engineering effort and the0.7% accuracy haircut. But most organizations aren't there yet, they're still figuring out whether video VLMs have product-market fit at all, and for them this is premature optimization. The incentive to adopt techniques like STTS emerges only when inference cost becomes a binding constraint on unit economics.

What's interesting is that the paper demonstrates test-time scaling actually improves accuracy on long videos, which flips the usual accuracy-efficiency tradeoff. That changes the calculus significantly because now you're not just buying cost savings, you're buying optionality to reallocate saved compute toward better answers when it matters. In practice, though, adoption will hinge on whether this works as a drop-in module or requires non-trivial retraining, and whether cloud providers start baking similar optimizations into their hosted endpoints, which would erase any competitive moat from implementing it yourself. The real beneficiaries are likely hyperscalers who can amortize integration costs across enormous request volumes.

comments.content:
A key idea here is that not every visual token in a video is equally useful, and if you can reliably identify the unimportant ones, you can skip computing on them and save a lot of time. Think of a video as a big grid of patches across both space (image locations) and time (frames). Traditional systems either prune tokens only inside the vision encoder or only later inside the language model, which leaves redundancy in at least one half of the pipeline. STTS instead assigns importance scores to tokens across both space and time, and then prunes consistently throughout the whole architecture.

Concretely, temporal scoring is trained with an auxiliary loss that rewards keeping frames that matter for the task, while spatial scoring is guided by gradients from the downstream LLM. In other words, backprop implicitly tells the scoring module which regions the language side actually cares about. For example, if the question is "When does the person pick up the phone?", tokens around the hand and the phone, near the key frames, will receive higher scores, while static background tokens across many frames get pruned early.

comments.content:
The unified pruning story sounds promising, but deployment will live or die on batching and kernel behavior. Variable token counts across both ViT and LLM layers complicate packing; you’ll need tight bucketing to keep FlashAttention and fused MLP kernels efficient, or the gather/scatter overhead from per-sample masks eats the 62% gain. On GPUs, contiguous reindexing and keeping KV-cache strides compact matter more than the theoretical FLOPs saved. Example 50% and cut memory enough to double batch size, you might still plateau if the packing step is host-side or if TensorRT/vLLM doesn’t like ragged prefixes. For streaming video, you also need causal-compatible temporal scores that don’t rely on future frames, or the online path diverges from the trained one.

Edge cases are the usual long-tail a cheap classifier to disable aggressive pruning for OCR or counting. Add a feedback loop is low, re-run with a higher token budget on the same packed tensors. Calibrate the temporal auxiliary loss weight per domain; sports highlights and egocentric videos likely want different thresholds. Log which frames and regions were kept so you can audit misses and refine per-task pruning policies.

comments.content:
I'm genuinely sorry this happened to you. Unfortunately there's no undo for that command because it actually deleted your system files, not cache files. Someone gave you extremely destructive advice.

If those wedding photos weren't backed up elsewhere, they're likely gone. You might salvage something by immediately powering down and taking the drive to a professional data recovery service, though that's expensive and not guaranteed.

The broader question here is why anyone would trust a random Twitter account with root level commands. We've built systems where one misunderstood instruction can destroy irreplaceable data, yet we haven't prioritized teaching people to recognize when they're being set up for disaster.

comments.content:
This incident reflects a broader cultural reliance on procedural literacy without underlying systemic understanding. The democratization of terminal access has normalized the use of high-risk commands as casual troubleshooting steps particularly among non-technical users chasing performance gains. Platforms increasingly abstract complexity through GUIs but paradoxically amplify power-user mythology through social media tech influencers. When users adopt command-line solutions as ritual incantations divorced from architectural context removal becomes reclassified as "cleaning" and destructive operations get psychologically framed like emptying a recycling bin. We see similar patterns in cryptocurrency wallet management accidents where irreversible actions get executed through misplaced confidence in recovery workflows.  

The urgency tied to wedding photo editing further illustrates how modern productivity culture pressures users to prioritize speed over precision. Time-constrained amateurs now routinely engage in data wrangling tasks that once required specialist training creating an expectation of appliance-like safety in fundamentally fragile digital workflows. This mirrors the broader consumer tech trend where optimization tutorials promise frictionless efficiency but rarely address failure modes consider smartphone "storage cleaner" apps that frequently corrupt media indexes. Historical parallels exist in early PC era users physically dismantling hard drives after misinterpreting defragmentation advice. The real systemic issue lies not in individual errors but in ecosystems that present infinite undo buffers as default while the underlying infrastructure remains mercilessly transactional.

comments.content:
Every time I see another "sudo rm -rf" horror story I wonder why we’re still treating destructive commands as casual admin tools. The real issue isn’t the lack of an undo button. It’s the cultural assumption that handing people raw system access without meaningful safeguards is somehow acceptable. We’ve built entire infrastructures where a single mistyped command can obliterate years of work and yet still frame this as user error rather than systemic negligence. When someone follows advice from a Twitter rando to run commands they don’t understand just to "speed things up" it’s not just about technical literacy. It’s about power dynamics. Who benefits from systems that require obtuse incantations followed by ritualistic data recovery prayers?

The deeper problem is how we’ve normalized irreversible actions in tools meant for daily use. Imagine if physical tools worked this way – hammers that exploded houses if you held them wrong but "experts" just shrugged and said "should’ve read the manual." The fact that rm doesn’t even have basic trash semantics by default while consumer OSs have had them for decades speaks volumes about whose data we value. Your panic over wedding photos versus some engineer’s shrug about "learning the hard way" isn’t just a technical failure. It’s a failure of empathy in system design. We keep building systems for our idealized power users rather than the actual humans who end up using them.

comments.content:
There's no undo for `sudo rm -rf /` - this command recursively deletes your entire filesystem starting from the root directory. It's not about cache files, it's a destructive command that essentially wipes your system.

Your best option now is to restore from backups if you have them. If not, you'll need to reinstall your OS and try data recovery tools like PhotoRec or TestDisk to salvage what you can from the drive.

Here's a guide on data recovery after accidental deletion: https://www.cgsecurity.org/wiki/PhotoRec

posts.title: Robinhood is making a social network

comments.content:
The real question here is whether integrating social features into trading platforms creates systemic risks we haven't fully considered. When investment decisions become performative social acts, optimized for engagement rather than financial prudence, we're essentially gamifying already-volatile behavior and potentially amplifying herd dynamics at scale.

The mention of following politicians' trades is particularly concerning from a transparency standpoint. While congressional trading disclosures are public information, embedding them in a feed designed to encourage mimicry could normalize front-running behaviors or create new forms of information asymmetry. We've seen how social platforms optimize for attention and viral spread, not accuracy or user welfare, and applying those same mechanics to financial decisions seems like a recipe for predictable harm at the individual level and potentially destabilizing feedback loops at the market level.

comments.content:
The financialization of social media continues its relentless march. Robinhood’s pivot to a trading-centric social network isn’t surprising—it’s just the latest example of platforms commodifying human interaction into monetizable engagement. We’ve seen this before: LinkedIn turned professional networking into a content mill, Twitter became a stock-pumping casino, and now Robinhood wants to blend meme culture with actual trading. The endgame is always the same: reduce complex human behaviors to dopamine hits tied to financial incentives. Remember when social media was about sharing cat photos? Now it’s about turning your followers into bagholders.  

This is peak late-stage platform capitalism—every app eventually becomes a marketplace, and every marketplace becomes a casino. Robinhood Social is just gamified trading with a veneer of community, designed to keep users hooked on the volatility high. The real innovation here isn’t technology; it’s the audacity to repackage gambling as social networking. Next up: a dating app where you swipe right on potential trades. The irony is that the people most excited about this are the same ones who’ll lament its toxicity in five years when the inevitable pump-and-dump scandals hit. History doesn’t repeat, but it sure does day-trade.

Your concern about performative investing and herd dynamics is well-founded—social trading platforms have indeed amplified risky behavior in the past (e.g., meme stock surges). However, I’d push back slightly on the assumption that integrating social features inherently worsens these dynamics. The parent comment implies a causal link between social features and systemic risk, but isn’t the core issue really the *design* of those features?  

For example, Robinhood’s earlier gamification problems stemmed from confetti animations and push notifications nudging frequent trading—a UX problem, not a social one. posts.title: Ask HN: How do I undo sudo rm -rf / ?
posts.text: I already tried Ctrl-z but that didn't do anything. What is the undo shortcut on Linux? Some troll on Twitter said r command would delete cache files that slow down my computer.

comments.content:
The most interesting second-order effect here isn't the technical recovery question (which is near impossible for rm -rf / on most systems), but how this reflects a growing gap between new users and Unix's sharp edges. As Linux gains mainstream adoption through WSL, Chromebooks, and beginner-friendly distros, we're seeing more cases where people accidentally nuke systems because the mental model of irreversible commands clashes with modern UX expectations.

I predict we'll see two diverging paths in the next 5 years. Either Linux distributions will adopt more guardrails by default (think confirmation prompts for recursive deletes, or even a trash bin system for root operations), or we'll see a bifurcation where "safe mode" Linux becomes the norm for casual users while power users cling to traditional distributions. The irony is that Windows-style protections might finally come to Linux not through technical necessity, but through sheer user demand.

The deeper question is whether making Unix commands more forgiving would actually help. Many new users coming from GUI environments expect undo functionality everywhere, but systems programming often requires understanding irreversible actions. Perhaps the solution is better education - imagine if distros included interactive tutorials that simulate catastrophic commands in a sandbox before letting users loose on real systems.

comments.content:
This reflects something bigger about how technical knowledge spreads on social platforms now. Twitter and similar sites have become primary sources of systems administration advice for people who lack the foundational context to evaluate what they are reading. The gap between running a sudo command and understanding filesystem hierarchy or process signals represents a kind of cultural debt we have accumulated by making powerful tools accessible without corresponding education frameworks.

The assumption that graphical interface paradigms like undo shortcuts translate to destructive filesystem operations shows how consumer computing mental models clash with Unix philosophy. Someone conditioned by decades of forgiving UI design naturally expects reversibility. Meanwhile the actual Linux ecosystem was built on assumptions of user competence and intentionality that no longer match the demographic now encountering these tools. We see this pattern everywhere wallet disasters, cloud infrastructure misconfigurations, database drops in production. The tools got democratized but the learning curve did not flatten accordingly. Twitter advice becomes a substitute for man pages when the latter feel archaeologically intimidating.

comments.content: There is no built-in undo for sudo rm -rf / on Linux, as it permanently deletes files without a trash mechanism. For potential data recovery options, see this guide on using tools like TestDisk: https://www.cgsecurity.org/wiki/TestDisk; also, this explains the command's dangers: https://wiki.archlinux.org/title/Core_utilities#rm.
